12 Stones Global · Kilo Aupuni · govOS · public service

Title 12 — Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places Plain-Language Service Free · no account

A plain-language navigator to Maui County Code Title 12 (Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places): when you need a permit to work in the public right-of-way (excavating a street, cutting a curb, building a driveway, placing anything in a public place), how the process works, and who pays. Administered by Public Works & Waste Management; permits go through MAPPS. Sections marked expanding are being filled in; nothing here is legal advice.

★ Parcel lookup live data

Planning work that touches the street, curb, or sidewalk in front of your property? Enter a Maui County TMK to see the parcel and its frontage context from the Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. The road right-of-way itself is County-owned — most work in it needs a Title 12 permit, applied through MAPPS.

Tip: type the digits of your TMK (zone-section-plat-parcel). Don't know it? Open the State Land Use Viewer ↗ to find it on a map.

Live designations: Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS. Right-of-way permits via Maui County MAPPS (Public Works). No parcel data is stored or fabricated — fetched live. County ROW / road-ownership overlay

1 · Work in the public right-of-way sourced

The general path for permitted work in a County street, sidewalk, or public place under Title 12.

  1. Get a written permit first
    No one may break up, dig up, disturb, undermine, or dig under any County highway, street, alley, sidewalk, or other public place without a written permit from Public Works & Waste Management.
    Expense: permit fee · paid by: applicant
  2. Driveways & curb cuts
    No one may construct, reconstruct, or repair a driveway, or cut or lower a curb on a County right-of-way, without a written permit from the director (Ch. 12.08).
    Expense: permit fee + construction · paid by: applicant
  3. Work safely & protect the public
    The permit holder must use barricades, lights, and watchmen as needed for safety, and may not leave debris, materials, or equipment that creates a hazard.
    Expense: safety measures · paid by: permit holder
  4. Restore the right-of-way
    On completion, the permit holder must restore the government right-of-way to good condition; indemnification applies (§12.08.140).
    Expense: restoration · paid by: permit holder
⚖️ Position — James Langford (analysis, not law; a stance beside the rule)The right-of-way is shared public space. Requiring a resident to restore what they disturb is fair. But where the County uses a frontage permit to require a resident to build public improvements that benefit everyone (a new sidewalk, drainage), the cost of the public benefit should be County-borne, not exacted from the one property owner.

Maui County Code Title 12 — excavation permit requirement + Ch. 12.08 (Driveways), §12.08.140; apply via MAPPS — Permits ↗. ROW restoration standards & bonding

2 · Which Title 12 permit? sourced

Common right-of-way activities and where to apply.

WorkPermitWhere
Excavate / cut a County street or sidewalkExcavation / street-opening permitMAPPS ↗
Build / repair a driveway, cut a curbDriveway permit — Ch. 12.08MAPPS ↗
Place objects / encroach into a public placeEncroachment — Title 12Title 12 ↗
Sidewalk in a special improvement areasee Ch. 12.42Ch. 12.42 ↗

Maui County Code Title 12 (§§12.02–12.54); permits via MAPPS / Public Works. full permit-type list + fees

3 · Enforcement sourced

Right-of-way violations are reachable through the County enforcement engine. Not legal advice.

Title 19's enforcement chapter, §19.530.030, expressly reaches violations of MCC titles 8, 12, 14, 16, 18, 19 and 20 plus related rules and permits — so unpermitted work in the right-of-way (an unauthorized excavation, driveway, or encroachment under Title 12) can draw a notice of violation and order requiring removal/restoration and/or civil fines, final after 30 days unless appealed to the Board of Variances and Appeals.

Maui County Code Ch. 19.530 (§19.530.030 reach, sourced in the Title 19 service) + Title 12 permit conditions. Title 12-specific penalty provisions

4 · Who pays — the charter lens analysis

Labeled analysis — the right-of-way read through two charters. Not adopted law; an interpretive lens beside the Code.

Maui County Charter — lensUnder the Maui County Charter, Public Works manages and maintains the County's streets, sidewalks, and public places and permits work within them; permit fees and restoration requirements protect the public asset. The Charter frames the ROW as a public infrastructure the department stewards.
Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) — lensUnder the Sovereign Charter (12 Stones) lens, the street and sidewalk are a commons held in trust for all. Permitting is legitimate to protect that commons; the lens distinguishes restoring what you disturb (the resident's duty) from building new public infrastructure as a permit condition (the County's duty), and resists shifting the latter onto a single owner.
⚖️ Position — analysis, not lawRestore what you break — fair. Fund the public sidewalk the whole neighborhood uses — that's the County's job, not a toll on whoever happens to pull a frontage permit. Same right-of-way, read two ways.

5 · Rules → Code: candidates for codification analysis

⚖️ Analysis / position — not lawProvisions that, in our assessment, Title 12 should make clearer or fairer so residents know their cost and duty. Citations are verbatim-checked; the recommendation is a stance beside the rule.
  • Publish ROW permit fees + restoration standards. → Codify a single readable fee + restoration-spec table so an applicant can price the work up front. expanding
  • Separate restore-vs-improve. Distinguish restoration (applicant) from new public improvements (County) so frontage permits aren't used to download public infrastructure onto residents.
  • Encroachment clarity. Codify clear, plain-language rules for what may and may not occupy a public place, so enforcement is predictable.

Analysis grounded in MCC Title 12; contents expanding pending verbatim ingest.

Free now (no account): this page, the live parcel lookup, the ROW navigator, and the permit table are all Tier 1 — free. Coming — Tier 3 (verified tools): a right-of-way permit-readiness checklist and an AI Title-12 assistant will be verification-gated (identity-verified). Identity is verified through the payment processor — only verified-status is stored here, never the raw ID. No live billing is enabled (Stripe keys are environment references only); the actual permit application happens on MAPPS.
govOS · Kilo Aupuni · 12 Stones Global · free public service · sourced to Maui County Code Title 12 (Streets, Sidewalks & Public Places) via Municode + County of Maui Public Works & Waste Management + MAPPS (official permitting) + Hawaiʻi Statewide GIS · first version 2026-06-18, expanding · Built by the County Service Module Engine (config + sourced content — no bespoke page) · enforcement reach per MCC §19.530.030 · not legal advice · public records only · hands off to MAPPS for the official record.
